With a nearly uniform temperature of 2.725 Kelvin, it permeates all of space and arrives equally from all directions. It represents the earliest snapshot of the universe, from when it was just 380,000 years old — about 1/37,000th of its current age.

The CMB is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence for the Big Bang model of the universe's origin and evolution. Its discovery in 1965, detailed mapping, and precise measurement of its tiny temperature fluctuations have provided the foundation for modern precision cosmology.

sections.title: Discovery
body: The CMB was predicted in the 1940s by George Gamow, Ralph Alpher, and Robert Herman as a consequence of the Big Bang model — the early universe was so hot and dense that it was opaque, but as it expanded and cooled, it became transparent, releasing radiation that should still be detectable today.

In 1965, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, engineers at Bell Telephone Laboratories in New Jersey, detected an unexplained microwave noise in their horn antenna that was the same in all directions and consistent throughout the year. They were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1978 for this discovery, which was made somewhat serendipitously.

sections.title: The Last Scattering Surface
body: In the early universe, the plasma of protons, neutrons, and electrons was so hot and dense that photons were constantly scattered — the universe was opaque to light, like the interior of the Sun. As the universe expanded and cooled to about 3,000 K, electrons combined with protons to form neutral hydrogen atoms in an event called recombination.

With neutral atoms formed, photons could travel freely for the first time. This 'last scattering surface' — the moment the universe became transparent — is what we see when we observe the CMB. The light has been traveling toward us ever since, stretched by the universe's expansion from the visible to microwave wavelengths.

sections.title: Temperature Fluctuations
body: Although the CMB is extremely uniform, precise measurements reveal tiny temperature fluctuations — variations of about 1 part in 100,000. These fluctuations were imprinted by quantum fluctuations in the very early universe that were stretched to macroscopic scales by a period of exponential expansion called cosmic inflation.

These primordial density variations acted as seeds for all the large-scale structure in the universe — the fluctuations grew under gravity over billions of years to become galaxies, galaxy clusters, and the cosmic web. The angular scale, amplitude, and pattern of CMB fluctuations encode the fundamental parameters of the universe.

sections.title: Planck Mission Results
body: ESA's Planck satellite (2009–2013) produced the most detailed maps of the CMB ever made. Planck's 2018 results constrained the universe's age to 13.801 billion years, its Hubble constant to 67.4 km/s/Mpc, ordinary matter at 4.9%, dark matter at 26.8%, and dark energy at 68.3% of total energy with extraordinary precision.

sections.title: CMB Polarization and Inflation
body: The CMB is not only characterized by its temperature variations but also by subtle patterns of polarization — the orientation of the electric field of the microwave photons. There are two types: E-mode polarization (produced by density fluctuations) and B-mode polarization (produced by gravitational waves from inflation).

Detecting the primordial B-mode signal from inflationary gravitational waves is considered the 'holy grail' of CMB science. A detection would provide direct evidence for inflation and constrain models of the very early universe. Current and future experiments (BICEP/Keck, CMB-S4, LiteBIRD) are pursuing this goal.

sections.title: The Hubble Tension
body: One of the most significant current problems in cosmology is the Hubble Tension — a discrepancy between the value of the Hubble constant (the universe's current expansion rate) measured from the CMB and the early universe versus measurements using local distance indicators like Cepheid variables and supernovae.

The CMB-based value (from Planck) is ~67 km/s/Mpc, while local measurements consistently give ~73 km/s/Mpc. This ~5σ discrepancy is unlikely to be due to measurement error and may indicate new physics beyond the standard ΛCDM cosmological model — possibly early dark energy, new relativistic species, or other modifications.

Space.summary: The search for extraterrestrial life is one of the most profound and captivating scientific endeavors in human history. It encompasses the search for microbial life on worlds within our Solar System, the detection of biosignatures in exoplanet atmospheres, and the search for technological civilizations through the Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI).
Space.title: The Search for Extraterrestrial Life
Space.subtitle: Are we alone? — The scientific quest to find life beyond Earth

sections.title: Overview
body: The search for extraterrestrial life is one of the most profound and captivating scientific endeavors in human history. It encompasses the search for microbial life on worlds within our Solar System, the detection of biosignatures in exoplanet atmospheres, and the search for technological civilizations through the Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI).

Astrobiology — the study of the origin, evolution, and distribution of life in the universe — draws on biology, chemistry, planetary science, astronomy, and geology. Despite decades of searching, no confirmed evidence for extraterrestrial life has been found. Yet the scientific case that life could exist elsewhere has never been stronger.

sections.title: Life in the Solar System
body: Mars is the most studied candidate for past or present life. Mars had liquid water on its surface billions of years ago, including rivers, lakes, and possibly an ocean. NASA's Perseverance rover is drilling cores from an ancient lakebed in Jezero Crater, searching for preserved biosignatures and collecting samples for eventual return to Earth.

Jupiter's moon Europa and Saturn's moon Enceladus are considered the most promising targets for present-day life. Both harbor liquid water oceans beneath their icy shells. Enceladus actively vents water vapor and organic molecules into space through geysers — sampled by the Cassini spacecraft — and NASA's Europa Clipper mission (launched October 2024) will study Europa's habitability in detail.

sections.title: Titan: A World of Organic Chemistry
body: Saturn's moon Titan has a thick atmosphere of nitrogen and methane, with liquid methane lakes and rivers on its surface — the only body besides Earth with a stable liquid on its surface. While too cold for water-based life, some scientists speculate about the possibility of exotic life using liquid methane as a solvent. NASA's Dragonfly rotorcraft lander is planned to explore Titan in the 2030s.

sections.title: The Conditions for Life
body: Life as we know it requires liquid water, a source of energy (chemical or from a star), and organic chemistry based on carbon. These requirements guide the search for habitable environments. However, life might also exist in forms very different from life on Earth, using different solvents, energy sources, or even different biochemistries.

Extremophiles — organisms that thrive in extreme conditions (extreme temperature, pressure, salinity, radiation, acidity) on Earth — have expanded scientists' understanding of habitable niches. Life has been found in Antarctic ice, deep-sea hydrothermal vents, hypersaline lakes, highly acidic rivers, and even in the stratosphere.

sections.title: SETI: The Search for Intelligent Life
body: SETI (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) seeks evidence of technological civilizations through the detection of signals — typically radio or laser signals — from space. The first modern SETI search, Project Ozma, was conducted by Frank Drake in 1960, pointing a radio telescope at two nearby sun-like stars.

The Drake Equation, formulated by Frank Drake in 1961, is a probabilistic framework for estimating the number of communicative extraterrestrial civilizations in the Milky Way. It includes factors like the rate of star formation, the fraction with planets, the fraction with life, and the fraction that develop technological civilizations.

sections.title: The Wow! Signal
body: On August 15, 1977, astronomer Jerry Ehman detected a strong narrowband radio signal while working on a SETI project at Ohio State University's Big Ear radio telescope. He circled the signal and wrote 'Wow!' in the margin. The signal lasted 72 seconds and matched characteristics expected of a signal from deep space, but has never been detected again. It remains one of the most intriguing and unexplained observations in SETI history.

sections.title: The Fermi Paradox
body: The Fermi Paradox is the apparent contradiction between the high probability estimates for extraterrestrial civilizations and the lack of evidence for, or contact with, such civilizations. Physicist Enrico Fermi famously asked in 1950, 'Where is everybody?' — if intelligent life is common, why have we not detected any signs?

Proposed resolutions include the Great Filter hypothesis (a developmental hurdle that most or all life fails to pass, either in our past or in our future), the Zoo hypothesis (advanced civilizations deliberately avoid contact), the Rare Earth hypothesis (complex multicellular life is extremely rare), and the simple possibility that we have not searched long or thoroughly enough.

sections.title: Biosignatures and Future Searches
body: A biosignature is any substance or phenomenon that provides scientific evidence of past or present life. In exoplanet research, potential atmospheric biosignatures include oxygen (O2), ozone (O3), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), and phosphine (PH3) — though all require careful interpretation as they can be produced abiotically.

The James Webb Space Telescope is already characterizing exoplanet atmospheres with unprecedented detail. Future missions like the Habitable Worlds Observatory could detect Earth-like biosignatures in exoplanet atmospheres. The convergence of exoplanet science, solar system exploration, advances in synthetic biology, and powerful new telescopes makes the coming decades potentially transformative for the search for life.

Science.summary: The scientific method is a systematic approach to building knowledge through observation, hypothesis formation, experimentation, and analysis. It is the foundation of modern science and distinguishes scientific knowledge from belief, speculation, or authority. By requiring that claims be testable, reproducible, and subject to revision, the scientific method has produced humanity's most reliable and powerful body of knowledge.
Science.title: The Scientific Method
Science.subtitle: The engine of discovery — how humanity systematically builds reliable knowledge about the world

sections.title: Overview
body: The scientific method is a systematic approach to building knowledge through observation, hypothesis formation, experimentation, and analysis. It is the foundation of modern science and distinguishes scientific knowledge from belief, speculation, or authority. By requiring that claims be testable, reproducible, and subject to revision, the scientific method has produced humanity's most reliable and powerful body of knowledge.

There is no single rigid 'scientific method' — different sciences use different approaches. Physics relies heavily on mathematical models and controlled experiments; geology and paleontology depend on field observation of the rock record; evolutionary biology uses comparative anatomy, genetics, and the fossil record; epidemiology uses statistical analysis of populations. What unites them is a commitment to evidence-based reasoning and rigorous testing.

sections.title: Steps of the Scientific Method
body: Observation is the starting point — noticing a phenomenon that requires explanation. A well-framed question focuses inquiry. A hypothesis is a testable, falsifiable proposed explanation — not merely a guess but an informed prediction derived from prior knowledge. A hypothesis must make specific, testable predictions that could in principle be proven wrong.

Experimental design involves creating controlled conditions to test the hypothesis, manipulating one variable (independent variable) while measuring its effect on another (dependent variable) and controlling all other factors. Data collection and analysis — including statistical testing — determine whether results support or contradict the hypothesis. Conclusions are drawn and communicated, inviting scrutiny and replication by others.

sections.title: Falsifiability and Popper
body: Philosopher Karl Popper argued in the 1930s that the key feature distinguishing scientific hypotheses from non-scientific claims is falsifiability — a genuine scientific claim must be capable of being proven false by evidence. A hypothesis that is compatible with any possible observation is not scientifically useful. This criterion helps distinguish science from pseudoscience.

For example, 'all swans are white' is falsifiable — one black swan disproves it. 'The universe was created to appear as if it has a natural history' is not falsifiable — no observation could refute it. Falsifiability does not mean a scientific idea is wrong or likely to be disproven — it means we know what evidence would falsify it, which is a strength, not a weakness.

sections.title: Peer Review and Replication
body: Peer review is the process by which scientific manuscripts are evaluated by independent experts before publication. Reviewers assess the validity of methodology, soundness of reasoning, and appropriateness of conclusions. While imperfect, peer review is a crucial quality-control mechanism that filters out errors, bias, and fraud.

Replication — the ability of independent researchers to reproduce results — is a cornerstone of scientific reliability. A finding that cannot be replicated is suspect regardless of its initial impressiveness. A 'replication crisis' identified in the 2010s, particularly in psychology and medicine, revealed that many published findings could not be reproduced, prompting reform in research practices, statistical standards, and publication incentives.

sections.title: Scientific Consensus vs Controversy
body: Scientific consensus — the collective judgment of active researchers in a field — carries great weight. Consensus on human-caused climate change, vaccine safety, evolution, and the age of the universe is based on converging lines of evidence from independent research programs worldwide. Distinguishing genuine scientific controversy (active expert debate about mechanisms or specifics) from manufactured controversy (misrepresented to the public by vested interests) is essential for science literacy.

sections.title: Scientific Theories and Laws
body: In everyday language, 'theory' implies speculation. In science, a theory is a well-substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world, based on a body of evidence that has been repeatedly confirmed through observation and experimentation. Evolution, relativity, quantum mechanics, germ theory, and plate tectonics are all scientific theories — not guesses but powerful, predictive explanatory frameworks.

A scientific law describes a consistent, universal relationship (usually mathematical) between phenomena under specified conditions — like Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation or the Law of Conservation of Energy. Laws describe what happens; theories explain why. Contrary to a common misconception, theories do not 'graduate' into laws — they are different types of scientific knowledge.

sections.title: Paradigm Shifts
body: Philosopher Thomas Kuhn proposed in 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions' (1962) that science does not progress only by slow accumulation of facts but also through paradigm shifts — revolutionary changes in the fundamental assumptions and framework of a scientific field. Examples include the Copernican revolution (heliocentric vs geocentric solar system), Darwinian evolution, quantum mechanics, and plate tectonics.

During a scientific paradigm shift, anomalies that the old framework could not explain accumulate until a crisis point is reached, and a new paradigm emerges that better explains the full range of observations. The transition is often resisted by established scientists and can take decades, illustrating that science is a human enterprise subject to sociological as well as logical forces.

Science.summary: Newton's three laws of motion, published in his landmark work Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica in 1687, form the foundation of classical mechanics. They describe the relationship between a body's motion and the forces acting upon it, enabling precise prediction of the behavior of objects from falling apples to orbiting planets.
Science.title: Laws of Motion
Science.subtitle: Newton's three laws and beyond — the principles governing how objects move through the universe

sections.title: Overview
body: Newton's three laws of motion, published in his landmark work Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica in 1687, form the foundation of classical mechanics. They describe the relationship between a body's motion and the forces acting upon it, enabling precise prediction of the behavior of objects from falling apples to orbiting planets.

For over two centuries, Newton's laws were considered the complete description of motion. In the early 20th century, Albert Einstein's theories of special relativity (1905) and general relativity (1915) revealed their limitations at very high speeds and in very strong gravitational fields. Quantum mechanics showed further limitations at the atomic scale. Yet Newton's laws remain accurate and sufficient for the vast majority of everyday engineering and physics problems.

sections.title: Newton's First Law — Inertia
body: Newton's First Law states: 'An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.' This property of matter — its resistance to changes in its state of motion — is called inertia.

The concept of inertia fundamentally challenged Aristotle's view that objects naturally come to rest and require a continuous force to keep moving. In reality, friction and air resistance are forces that slow moving objects on Earth. In a frictionless vacuum, a moving object would continue indefinitely. The seatbelt in a car works because your body has inertia — when the car stops suddenly, your body tends to continue moving forward.

sections.title: Newton's Second Law — Force and Acceleration
body: Newton's Second Law states: 'The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass.' This is expressed as F = ma (Force = mass × acceleration). Force is measured in Newtons (N), where 1 N = 1 kg·m/s².

This law is perhaps the most quantitatively powerful of the three. It allows us to calculate exactly how an object will accelerate given its mass and the forces acting on it — or conversely, to calculate the force required to produce a desired acceleration. It explains why a truck requires much more force to accelerate than a bicycle, and why the same force produces less acceleration in a more massive object.

sections.title: Momentum and Impulse
body: Momentum (p = mv, mass × velocity) is a fundamental conserved quantity in physics. Newton's Second Law can be re-expressed as: the net force on an object equals the rate of change of its momentum. Impulse — the product of force and time — equals the change in momentum. This is why airbags in cars extend the collision time, reducing the peak force and the risk of injury even though the total momentum change is the same.

sections.title: Newton's Third Law — Action and Reaction
body: Newton's Third Law states: 'For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.' When object A exerts a force on object B, object B simultaneously exerts an equal and opposite force on object A. These forces always act on different objects — they do not cancel each other out.

A rocket engine expels gas downward (action); the gas pushes the rocket upward (reaction). When you walk, your foot pushes backward on the ground (action); the ground pushes your foot forward (reaction — the force that actually moves you). A gun recoils when fired because the bullet is pushed forward with the same momentum that the gun gains in the backward direction.

sections.title: Universal Gravitation
body: Alongside his laws of motion, Newton formulated the Law of Universal Gravitation: every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. F = G(m₁m₂)/r², where G is the gravitational constant.

This single equation unified the motion of falling objects on Earth with the orbital motion of the Moon and planets — a profound conceptual leap. It explained Kepler's three laws of planetary motion and remains accurate enough to navigate spacecraft throughout the Solar System. Only in the vicinity of very massive objects (black holes, neutron stars) does Einstein's general relativity produce significantly different predictions.

sections.title: Beyond Newton — Relativity and Quantum Mechanics
body: Einstein's special relativity (1905) showed that Newton's laws break down at speeds approaching the speed of light. At relativistic speeds, mass increases with velocity, time dilates, and lengths contract. The famous equation E = mc² shows that mass and energy are equivalent — a concept completely absent from Newtonian mechanics.

Quantum mechanics revealed that at the atomic and subatomic scale, particles do not follow definite trajectories as Newton's laws predict. Instead, they are described by probability wave functions. Nevertheless, classical mechanics emerges from quantum mechanics when applied to large numbers of particles — a correspondence principle ensuring Newton's laws remain valid and indispensable for macroscopic objects.

Science.summary: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics concerned with heat, work, temperature, and their relation to energy, entropy, and the physical properties of matter. It arose in the 19th century from the practical need to understand and improve steam engines, and was rapidly recognized as one of the most fundamental branches of physics — its laws govern every physical and chemical process in the universe.
Science.title: Thermodynamics
Science.subtitle: The science of heat, work, and energy — the laws that govern every process in the universe

sections.title: Overview
body: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics concerned with heat, work, temperature, and their relation to energy, entropy, and the physical properties of matter. It arose in the 19th century from the practical need to understand and improve steam engines, and was rapidly recognized as one of the most fundamental branches of physics — its laws govern every physical and chemical process in the universe.

The four laws of thermodynamics set absolute limits on what is physically possible. They explain why heat flows only from hot to cold, why no engine can be perfectly efficient, why the universe is evolving toward greater disorder, and why absolute zero can be approached but never quite reached.

sections.title: The Zeroth Law — Thermal Equilibrium
body: The Zeroth Law states: 'If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.' This seemingly simple statement provides the conceptual basis for temperature as a measurable quantity and justifies the use of thermometers.

It was labeled 'zeroth' because it was recognized as more fundamental than the First and Second Laws, which had already been numbered, yet it had been assumed implicitly. The Zeroth Law establishes that temperature is a transitive property — a foundation for all thermometric measurements.

sections.title: The First Law — Conservation of Energy
body: The First Law of Thermodynamics is the law of conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems: 'The change in internal energy of a closed system equals the heat added to the system minus the work done by the system.' In equation form: ΔU = Q − W.

Energy cannot be created or destroyed — it can only be converted from one form to another. When you burn gasoline in a car engine, chemical energy is converted to thermal energy (heat), which is converted to mechanical energy (work), with some lost as heat to the surroundings. No device can produce more energy than it consumes — perpetual motion machines of the first kind (producing energy from nothing) are impossible.

sections.title: The Second Law — Entropy
body: The Second Law of Thermodynamics has several equivalent formulations: heat never spontaneously flows from a cold body to a hot body; no heat engine operating in a cycle can convert all its heat input into work; the entropy of an isolated system always increases or remains constant — it never decreases spontaneously.

Entropy (S) measures the dispersal of energy or the number of microscopic configurations available to a system. A room-temperature coffee cup that spontaneously heats up while the table cools would not violate energy conservation but would violate the Second Law — because it would decrease total entropy. The Second Law gives time its arrow — it explains why processes are irreversible and why the universe evolves in one direction.

sections.title: The Carnot Engine
body: Sadi Carnot showed in 1824 that the maximum efficiency of any heat engine operating between a hot reservoir (temperature Th) and a cold reservoir (Tc) is: η = 1 − Tc/Th. This Carnot efficiency is a fundamental upper limit — no real engine can exceed it. A steam turbine operating between 600°C and 30°C has a maximum theoretical efficiency of about 66%; real turbines achieve 40–50% due to irreversibilities.

sections.title: The Third Law — Absolute Zero
body: The Third Law states: 'As a system approaches absolute zero, all processes cease and the entropy of the system approaches a minimum constant value (zero for a perfect crystal).' It implies that absolute zero (0 Kelvin = −273.15°C) can never be reached in a finite number of steps — it can only be approached asymptotically.

Scientists have achieved temperatures within billionths of a Kelvin of absolute zero using laser cooling and magnetic evaporative cooling techniques. Near absolute zero, quantum mechanical effects dominate: matter enters exotic states like Bose-Einstein condensates (where many particles occupy the same quantum state) and superfluidity (frictionless flow).

sections.title: Thermodynamics in Everyday Life
body: Thermodynamics is pervasive in technology and nature. Power plants (coal, nuclear, gas) are heat engines, converting thermal energy to electricity with efficiencies governed by thermodynamics. Refrigerators and air conditioners are heat pumps — using work to move heat against its natural flow. Internal combustion engines in cars operate on thermodynamic cycles (Otto, Diesel).

Biological systems obey thermodynamics. Living organisms are open systems that import energy (food, sunlight) and export entropy (heat, waste) to maintain local order at the cost of increasing disorder in their surroundings. Life does not violate the Second Law — it is sustained by energy flow that increases global entropy, consistent with thermodynamic principles.

Science.summary: Electromagnetism is one of the four fundamental forces of nature. It describes the interactions between electrically charged particles and encompasses both electric and magnetic phenomena, which were shown to be aspects of a single unified force in the 19th century. Electromagnetism is responsible for virtually all the phenomena we encounter in daily life — light, chemical bonding, the solidity of matter, and all electrical and electronic technology.
Science.title: Electromagnetism
Science.subtitle: The unified force of electricity and magnetism — the foundation of modern technology

sections.title: Overview
body: Electromagnetism is one of the four fundamental forces of nature. It describes the interactions between electrically charged particles and encompasses both electric and magnetic phenomena, which were shown to be aspects of a single unified force in the 19th century. Electromagnetism is responsible for virtually all the phenomena we encounter in daily life — light, chemical bonding, the solidity of matter, and all electrical and electronic technology.

The unification of electricity and magnetism by James Clerk Maxwell in 1865, expressed in four elegant equations, was one of the greatest intellectual achievements in the history of science. Maxwell's equations predicted the existence of electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light — leading to the realization that light itself is an electromagnetic wave.

sections.title: Electric Force and Coulomb's Law
body: The electric force between charged particles was quantified by Charles-Augustin de Coulomb in 1785. Coulomb's Law states that the force between two point charges is proportional to the product of their charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them: F = k·q₁q₂/r². Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.

The electric field is a vector field that describes the force per unit charge at any point in space, produced by electric charges. Electric potential (voltage) is the potential energy per unit charge. Capacitors store energy in electric fields between parallel conducting plates — fundamental components in virtually all electronic circuits.

sections.title: Magnetic Force
body: Magnetism arises from the motion of electric charges — either macroscopic currents or the intrinsic quantum mechanical 'spin' of elementary particles. Moving charges create magnetic fields, and magnetic fields exert forces on moving charges (the Lorentz force). A current-carrying wire in a magnetic field experiences a force — the basis of electric motors.

Permanent magnets arise from the alignment of magnetic domains — regions where atomic magnetic moments are aligned. In most materials, domains are randomly oriented and cancel; in ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt), domains can be aligned by an external field and remain aligned. Earth's magnetic field is generated by currents in the liquid outer core, not by a permanent magnet.

sections.title: Maxwell's Equations
body: James Clerk Maxwell synthesized the work of Faraday, Gauss, and Ampère into four equations that completely describe classical electromagnetism. Gauss's Law relates electric fields to their sources (charges). Gauss's Law for Magnetism states there are no magnetic monopoles. Faraday's Law describes how changing magnetic fields create electric fields. Ampère-Maxwell Law shows that changing electric fields create magnetic fields.

Maxwell noticed that his equations predicted self-sustaining waves of oscillating electric and magnetic fields propagating through space. He calculated their speed as 299,792 km/s — matching the then-known speed of light. He boldly concluded that 'light is an electromagnetic disturbance propagated through the field according to electromagnetic laws' — unifying optics with electromagnetism.

sections.title: The Electromagnetic Spectrum
body: Electromagnetic radiation encompasses a vast spectrum of wavelengths and frequencies, all traveling at the speed of light in vacuum. From longest to shortest wavelength: radio waves (communication, astronomy), microwaves (radar, cooking, WiFi), infrared (heat, remote sensing, night vision), visible light (the narrow band our eyes detect), ultraviolet (causes sunburn, sterilization), X-rays (medical imaging, astronomy), and gamma rays (radioactive decay, nuclear reactions). All are the same phenomenon — oscillating electromagnetic fields — differing only in frequency.

sections.title: Electromagnetic Induction
body: Michael Faraday discovered in 1831 that a changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a conductor — electromagnetic induction. This principle is the basis for electric generators (converting mechanical motion to electricity), transformers (changing voltage levels), and electric motors (converting electricity to motion). These three devices underpin the entire modern electrical power system.

Faraday's discovery is embodied in Faraday's Law: the induced electromotive force (EMF) in a loop equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux through the loop. The negative sign reflects Lenz's Law: the induced current always acts to oppose the change producing it — a form of conservation of energy.

sections.title: Quantum Electrodynamics
body: Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) is the quantum field theory of electromagnetism, developed in the 1940s by Richard Feynman, Julian Schwinger, and Sin-Itiro Tomonaga (Nobel Prize, 1965). QED describes electromagnetic interactions as the exchange of virtual photons between charged particles and reconciles quantum mechanics with special relativity.

QED is the most precisely tested theory in all of science. Its prediction of the electron's magnetic moment agrees with experimental measurement to more than 10 significant figures — an accuracy comparable to measuring the distance from New York to Los Angeles to within the thickness of a human hair. This extraordinary precision makes QED the crowning achievement of 20th-century physics.

Science.summary: Quantum mechanics is the fundamental theory describing the behavior of matter and energy at the smallest scales — atoms, electrons, photons, and their interactions. It is the most successful and precisely tested physical theory ever devised, underpinning all of chemistry, much of physics, and the technology of modern civilization. Yet its implications for the nature of reality remain profoundly mysterious and philosophically contested.
Science.title: Quantum Mechanics
Science.subtitle: The strange and exact science of the very small — how the universe works at the atomic scale

sections.title: Overview
body: Quantum mechanics is the fundamental theory describing the behavior of matter and energy at the smallest scales — atoms, electrons, photons, and their interactions. It is the most successful and precisely tested physical theory ever devised, underpinning all of chemistry, much of physics, and the technology of modern civilization. Yet its implications for the nature of reality remain profoundly mysterious and philosophically contested.

Quantum mechanics emerged in the early 20th century as classical physics failed to explain phenomena like blackbody radiation, the photoelectric effect, and atomic spectra. The resulting theory is radically different from classical physics: particles have wave-like properties, outcomes are inherently probabilistic, observation affects the system being observed, and particles can be 'entangled' in ways that defy classical intuition.

sections.title: Wave-Particle Duality
body: One of the most startling discoveries of quantum mechanics is that light and matter exhibit both wave and particle properties, depending on how they are observed. In the double-slit experiment, electrons fired one at a time at a barrier with two slits create an interference pattern on a detector — behavior characteristic of waves. But when detectors are placed at the slits to observe which slit each electron passes through, the interference pattern disappears and electrons behave as classical particles.

The act of measurement — the interaction of the quantum system with a classical measuring device — determines which aspect (wave or particle) is revealed. Louis de Broglie proposed in 1924 that all matter has an associated wavelength: λ = h/p (wavelength equals Planck's constant divided by momentum). This 'matter wave' concept was confirmed by electron diffraction experiments.

sections.title: The Uncertainty Principle
body: Werner Heisenberg's uncertainty principle (1927) states that it is impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and exact momentum of a particle: Δx · Δp ≥ ℏ/2 (where ℏ = h/2π). The more precisely position is determined, the less precisely momentum can be known, and vice versa.

This is not a limitation of measurement technology but a fundamental property of nature. The uncertainty principle is a consequence of the wave nature of quantum particles — a localized wave packet inherently has a spread of wavelengths and therefore momenta. It has profound implications: atoms cannot collapse (electrons cannot simultaneously have zero position uncertainty and zero momentum), and the vacuum is not truly empty but seethes with quantum fluctuations.

sections.title: The Schrödinger Equation
body: Erwin Schrödinger formulated the equation governing how quantum states evolve in time (1926). The Schrödinger equation describes the wave function (Ψ) — a mathematical object encoding all information about a quantum system. The square of the wave function's magnitude gives the probability density of finding the particle at each location.

The wave function evolves smoothly and deterministically according to the Schrödinger equation — until a measurement is made. At the moment of measurement, the wave function 'collapses' to a definite state corresponding to the measured value. This 'measurement problem' — the apparent discontinuous jump in the wave function upon observation — is at the heart of the unresolved interpretational debates in quantum mechanics.

sections.title: Schrödinger's Cat
body: Schrödinger devised his famous cat thought experiment in 1935 to highlight the strangeness of quantum superposition. A cat in a sealed box is linked to a radioactive atom that may or may not decay. If the atom decays, it triggers a mechanism that kills the cat. Quantum mechanics seems to imply the atom is in a superposition of decayed and undecayed states until measured — and therefore the cat is simultaneously alive and dead until the box is opened. The paradox highlights the question of where the boundary between quantum superposition and definite classical reality lies.

sections.title: Quantum Entanglement
body: Quantum entanglement occurs when two particles interact in such a way that the quantum state of each cannot be described independently of the other, even when separated by large distances. Measuring a property of one particle instantly determines the corresponding property of the other — no matter how far apart they are.

Einstein called this 'spooky action at a distance' and believed it indicated quantum mechanics was incomplete. Bell's theorem (1964) and subsequent experiments (culminating in loophole-free tests in 2015) confirmed that quantum correlations cannot be explained by any 'local hidden variable' theory — entanglement is a real, non-classical feature of nature. It is the resource exploited by quantum cryptography and quantum computing.

sections.title: Quantum Applications
body: Despite its philosophical mysteries, quantum mechanics is the most practically successful physical theory ever developed. Transistors — the building blocks of all digital technology — operate on quantum tunneling and energy band theory. Lasers exploit stimulated emission of photons. MRI machines use nuclear magnetic resonance. Solar cells rely on the photoelectric effect. LEDs utilize quantum energy transitions in semiconductors.

Quantum computing promises exponential speedup for certain computational problems by exploiting superposition and entanglement. Quantum cryptography offers theoretically unbreakable encryption based on the laws of physics. Quantum sensors — atomic clocks, gravimeters, magnetometers — are revolutionizing navigation, medical imaging, and fundamental physics measurements.

Science.summary: The theory of evolution by natural selection is the central organizing principle of modern biology. It explains the diversity of life on Earth — the origin of species, adaptation, the pattern of biological relationships, and the fossil record — through a small number of simple, powerful mechanisms. As geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky wrote in 1973, 'Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution.'
Science.title: Theory of Evolution
Science.subtitle: Darwin's greatest idea — how natural selection shapes all life on Earth

sections.title: Overview
body: The theory of evolution by natural selection is the central organizing principle of modern biology. It explains the diversity of life on Earth — the origin of species, adaptation, the pattern of biological relationships, and the fossil record — through a small number of simple, powerful mechanisms. As geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky wrote in 1973, 'Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution.'

Biological evolution is the change in heritable characteristics of populations over successive generations. It is a fact that populations change over time and that all living organisms on Earth share a common ancestor. The theory of evolution explains how these changes occur — through natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, gene flow, and other processes.

sections.title: Natural Selection
body: Natural selection operates on three conditions: variation (individuals in a population vary in their heritable traits), heritability (traits are passed from parents to offspring), and differential fitness (some trait variants increase survival and reproductive success relative to others in the current environment). When these conditions are met, traits that enhance fitness become more common over generations.

Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace independently conceived natural selection in the mid-19th century. Darwin spent 20 years accumulating evidence before publishing On the Origin of Species in 1859. The book proposed that all species had descended with modification from common ancestors, driven primarily by natural selection — a process analogous to the artificial selection practiced by animal breeders but operating through survival and reproduction in nature.

sections.title: Evidence for Evolution
body: The fossil record documents the history of life, showing the successive appearance of organisms with increasing complexity and the gradual transformation of species over time. Transitional fossils — showing intermediate forms between major groups — include Tiktaalik (between fish and tetrapods), Archaeopteryx (between dinosaurs and birds), and numerous whale ancestors documenting the return to the sea.

Comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures — anatomical features of similar structure and origin in different species, consistent with common descent. The pentadactyl limb (five-digit limb) in humans, horses, bats, and whales differs dramatically in function but is structurally homologous, suggesting modification of a common ancestral structure. Vestigial structures (like the human coccyx or the hind limb remnants in snakes) are explained by evolution from ancestors in which the structures were functional.

sections.title: Molecular Evidence
body: Molecular biology has provided overwhelming independent evidence for evolution. DNA sequences of different species reveal the expected pattern of similarity based on evolutionary relationships — humans and chimpanzees share about 98.7% of their DNA sequence; the closer the evolutionary relationship, the more similar the genomes. Phylogenetic trees built from molecular data match those from morphology and the fossil record. Endogenous retroviruses — remnants of ancient viral infections integrated into the genome — found at the same positions in the genomes of related species confirm common descent.

sections.title: The Modern Evolutionary Synthesis
body: Darwin did not know the mechanism of heredity — he pre-dated Mendel's work on genetics, and DNA was not discovered until 1953. The Modern Evolutionary Synthesis (1930s–1940s) united Darwin's natural selection with Mendelian genetics, population genetics, and paleontology into a coherent framework.

Key figures included Ronald Fisher, J.B.S. Haldane, Sewall Wright (population genetics), Theodosius Dobzhansky (genetics and natural populations), Ernst Mayr (speciation and species concepts), and George Gaylord Simpson (paleontology and evolution). The synthesis established that mutations in DNA are the ultimate source of genetic variation, that natural selection is the primary driver of adaptation, and that species arise through the accumulation of genetic differences in isolated populations.

sections.title: Speciation and the Tree of Life
body: Speciation — the formation of new species — typically occurs when populations become reproductively isolated, preventing gene flow. Allopatric speciation occurs when a geographic barrier (mountain range, ocean) separates a population; the separated groups evolve independently until they can no longer interbreed if reunited. Sympatric speciation, still debated, involves speciation without geographic isolation.

The Tree of Life describes the evolutionary relationships of all organisms. Modern phylogenetics, using molecular data from thousands of genes, has resolved many relationships and revealed surprising ones: fungi are more closely related to animals than to plants; birds are living dinosaurs; and all complex (eukaryotic) cells contain ancient bacterial endosymbionts — the mitochondria.

sections.title: Human Evolution
body: Humans belong to the family Hominidae (great apes). Our closest living relatives are chimpanzees and bonobos, sharing a common ancestor approximately 6–7 million years ago. The hominin lineage (leading to humans) is characterized by bipedalism, expanding brain size, and complex tool use. Homo sapiens appeared in Africa approximately 300,000 years ago.

Ancient DNA analysis has revealed that modern humans interbred with Neanderthals (extinct ~40,000 years ago) and Denisovans, and that non-African humans today carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA. Human evolution continues — natural selection is still acting on human populations, and cultural and technological evolution now occur far faster than biological evolution.

Science.summary: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is the molecule that carries the genetic instructions for development, functioning, growth, and reproduction in all known living organisms and many viruses. DNA is a double helix — two intertwined polynucleotide strands, each composed of nucleotide units containing one of four nitrogen bases (adenine, thymine, guanine, cytosine), a deoxyribose sugar, and a phosphate group.
Science.title: DNA and Genetics
Science.subtitle: The code of life — how genetic information is stored, copied, and expressed

sections.title: Overview
body: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is the molecule that carries the genetic instructions for development, functioning, growth, and reproduction in all known living organisms and many viruses. DNA is a double helix — two intertwined polynucleotide strands, each composed of nucleotide units containing one of four nitrogen bases (adenine, thymine, guanine, cytosine), a deoxyribose sugar, and a phosphate group.

Genetics is the study of genes, heredity, and genetic variation in living organisms. It encompasses the mechanisms by which traits are inherited across generations (classical genetics), the molecular structure and function of genes (molecular genetics), the regulation of gene expression, and the application of genetic knowledge to medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology.

sections.title: The Double Helix
body: In 1953, James Watson and Francis Crick published the double helix structure of DNA, drawing on X-ray crystallography data produced by Rosalind Franklin and Raymond Gosling. The two DNA strands are anti-parallel and held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs: adenine always pairs with thymine (two hydrogen bonds) and guanine always pairs with cytosine (three hydrogen bonds). This complementary base pairing is the molecular basis of DNA replication and gene expression.

The DNA double helix is wound around histone proteins to form nucleosomes, which are further compacted into chromatin and ultimately into chromosomes. Human cells contain 46 chromosomes (23 pairs) in the nucleus. The total length of DNA in a single human cell, if stretched out, would be approximately 2 meters — yet it is packaged into a nucleus only about 6 micrometers in diameter.

sections.title: DNA Replication
body: Before a cell divides, its entire genome must be accurately copied. DNA replication is semi-conservative: the double helix unwinds, and each strand serves as a template for the synthesis of a new complementary strand. The enzyme DNA polymerase reads the template strand and adds complementary nucleotides, producing two identical daughter double helices.

The error rate of DNA polymerase is approximately 1 mistake per billion base pairs copied, thanks to proofreading mechanisms. Additional repair pathways correct damage from UV radiation, chemicals, and oxidative stress. Despite these safeguards, mutations accumulate over time — some are harmful, some neutral, and occasionally some are beneficial, providing the raw material for evolution.

sections.title: From Gene to Protein
body: The central dogma of molecular biology describes the flow of genetic information: DNA is transcribed into messenger RNA (mRNA), which is translated into protein. Transcription occurs in the nucleus: RNA polymerase reads the DNA template and synthesizes a complementary mRNA strand. The mRNA is processed (introns removed, exons spliced) and transported to the cytoplasm.

Translation occurs at ribosomes: the mRNA sequence is read in triplets (codons), each corresponding to one of 20 amino acids according to the genetic code. Transfer RNA (tRNA) molecules bring the correct amino acid to the ribosome for each codon. The chain of amino acids is assembled into a polypeptide that folds into a functional protein. There are ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes in the human genome, but over 100,000 distinct proteins, produced through alternative splicing and post-translational modification.

sections.title: Gene Expression and Regulation
body: Not all genes are active in every cell at every time. Gene expression is tightly regulated by transcription factors (proteins that bind to specific DNA sequences near genes), enhancers, silencers, and epigenetic mechanisms (modifications to DNA or histones that affect accessibility without changing the sequence). A liver cell and a neuron have the same DNA but express very different sets of genes — regulation of gene expression is what makes cell differentiation possible.

sections.title: Mendelian Genetics
body: Gregor Mendel, an Austrian monk, conducted experiments on pea plants from 1856–1863, crossing plants with different traits and counting offspring. He deduced two fundamental principles: the Law of Segregation (each organism carries two alleles for each trait, which separate during gamete formation so each gamete carries one) and the Law of Independent Assortment (alleles for different traits are inherited independently of each other, for traits on different chromosomes).

Mendel's work was rediscovered in 1900 and recognized as the foundation of classical genetics. Concepts of dominant and recessive alleles, genotype versus phenotype, homozygous versus heterozygous, and Punnett squares for predicting offspring ratios all derive from his insights. Exceptions to Mendel's laws (incomplete dominance, codominance, polygenic inheritance, gene linkage) were gradually worked out over the following decades.

sections.title: CRISPR and Genetic Engineering
body: CRISPR-Cas9 is a revolutionary genome-editing tool adapted from a bacterial immune system, developed by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 2020). It acts as molecular scissors — guided by a short RNA sequence to any specific location in the genome, where the Cas9 enzyme cuts the DNA. The cell's repair machinery then either disrupts the gene or incorporates new genetic material.

CRISPR has transformed biology research, making gene editing faster, cheaper, and more precise than ever before. Applications include creating animal models of disease, developing new therapies for genetic disorders (clinical trials for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia have shown dramatic results), engineering crops, and combating infectious disease. The 2018 announcement of the birth of gene-edited babies by He Jiankui sparked global ethical debate about the boundaries of human genetic modification.

Science.summary: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Cell theory — established by Matthias Schleiden, Theodor Schwann, and Rudolf Virchow in the 19th century — states that all living things are made of one or more cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and all cells arise from pre-existing cells.
Science.title: Cell Structure
Science.subtitle: The fundamental unit of life — inside the microscopic building blocks of all living things

sections.title: Overview
body: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Cell theory — established by Matthias Schleiden, Theodor Schwann, and Rudolf Virchow in the 19th century — states that all living things are made of one or more cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and all cells arise from pre-existing cells.

There are two fundamentally different types of cells: prokaryotic cells (bacteria and archaea), which lack a membrane-bound nucleus and other organelles; and eukaryotic cells (found in animals, plants, fungi, and protists), which have a nucleus and complex internal organization. Despite their differences, all cells share common features: a cell membrane, DNA as genetic material, ribosomes for protein synthesis, and the same basic genetic code.

sections.title: The Cell Membrane
body: The cell membrane (plasma membrane) is a selectively permeable barrier that separates the cell's interior from its environment. It consists of a phospholipid bilayer — two layers of phospholipid molecules with hydrophilic (water-loving) heads facing outward and hydrophobic (water-fearing) tails facing inward. Proteins embedded in the membrane serve as channels, transporters, receptors, and structural supports.

The fluid mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson, 1972) describes the membrane as a dynamic, two-dimensional fluid in which proteins are embedded and free to move laterally. Cholesterol molecules between phospholipids regulate membrane fluidity. The selective permeability of the membrane allows it to control the passage of ions, nutrients, and waste products, maintaining the cell's internal environment.

sections.title: The Nucleus
body: The nucleus is the control center of the eukaryotic cell, containing the cell's DNA packaged into chromosomes. It is enclosed by the nuclear envelope — a double membrane perforated by nuclear pore complexes that regulate the transport of molecules between the nucleus and cytoplasm. The nucleolus, a dense region within the nucleus, is where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized.

The nucleus stores and protects the cell's genetic information, coordinates gene expression, and oversees DNA replication. When a cell divides (mitosis or meiosis), the nuclear envelope breaks down, chromosomes are segregated to the daughter cells, and new nuclear envelopes form around each set of chromosomes.

sections.title: Key Organelles
body: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell, generating ATP (the cell's energy currency) through cellular respiration. They have a double membrane with the inner membrane folded into cristae to maximize surface area for ATP synthesis. Mitochondria contain their own DNA and ribosomes — evidence of their origin as ancient endosymbiotic bacteria, incorporated into early eukaryotic cells ~1.5–2 billion years ago.

The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a network of membrane-enclosed channels. The rough ER (studded with ribosomes) synthesizes and processes proteins destined for secretion or membrane insertion. The smooth ER synthesizes lipids and detoxifies drugs. The Golgi apparatus is the cell's postal system — receiving proteins from the ER, modifying, sorting, and packaging them for delivery to their destinations.

sections.title: Plant Cell Organelles
body: Plant cells have additional organelles not found in animal cells. Chloroplasts — like mitochondria, of endosymbiotic bacterial origin — are the sites of photosynthesis, converting light energy to chemical energy (glucose). They contain chlorophyll and other pigments. The central vacuole is a large, fluid-filled organelle that provides turgor pressure (structural support for the plant without a skeleton) and stores water, nutrients, and waste products. The rigid cell wall (made of cellulose) provides additional structural support and protection.

sections.title: Cell Division
body: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two genetically identical daughter cells each with the full complement of chromosomes (diploid). It proceeds through prophase (chromosomes condense), metaphase (chromosomes align at the cell equator), anaphase (sister chromatids separate to opposite poles), and telophase/cytokinesis (cell divides).

Meiosis is cell division for sexual reproduction, producing four genetically diverse daughter cells each with half the chromosome number (haploid) — sperm and egg cells (gametes). Meiosis involves two rounds of division and includes genetic recombination (crossing over) in prophase I, which shuffles genetic material between homologous chromosomes, generating genetic diversity.

sections.title: Cellular Processes
body: Cellular respiration is the process by which cells break down glucose and other molecules to produce ATP. It occurs in three stages: glycolysis (cytoplasm), the Krebs cycle (mitochondrial matrix), and the electron transport chain (inner mitochondrial membrane). The complete oxidation of one glucose molecule yields approximately 30–32 ATP molecules.

Cells communicate through chemical signals: hormones, neurotransmitters, and local signaling molecules bind to cell surface or intracellular receptors, triggering signal transduction pathways that alter gene expression or cellular behavior. Cell signaling coordinates development, immune responses, metabolism, and virtually all physiological processes.

Science.summary: A chemical reaction is a process that leads to the transformation of one set of chemical substances (reactants) to another (products). Chemical reactions involve the breaking and forming of chemical bonds, rearranging atoms into new configurations. The substances produced have different chemical properties from the starting materials — distinguishing chemical reactions from physical changes.
Science.title: Chemical Reactions
Science.subtitle: The transformation of matter — how atoms rearrange to form new substances and release energy

sections.title: Overview
body: A chemical reaction is a process that leads to the transformation of one set of chemical substances (reactants) to another (products). Chemical reactions involve the breaking and forming of chemical bonds, rearranging atoms into new configurations. The substances produced have different chemical properties from the starting materials — distinguishing chemical reactions from physical changes.

Chemical reactions are fundamental to all processes in chemistry, biology, geology, and materials science. Photosynthesis, respiration, combustion, corrosion, cooking, drug action, and the synthesis of materials all involve chemical reactions. Understanding reaction mechanisms, energetics, and kinetics allows chemists to design new materials, medicines, fuels, and industrial processes.

sections.title: Types of Chemical Reactions
body: Synthesis (combination) reactions: two or more reactants combine to form a single product. A + B → AB. Example: iron + sulfur → iron sulfide. Decomposition reactions: a single compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances. AB → A + B. Example: hydrogen peroxide decomposes to water and oxygen gas.

Single displacement reactions: one element replaces another in a compound. A + BC → AC + B. Example: zinc displacing copper from copper sulfate solution. Double displacement (metathesis) reactions: ions from two compounds exchange partners. AB + CD → AD + CB. Combustion reactions: a substance reacts rapidly with oxygen, producing heat and light. Acid-base reactions involve proton transfer; redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions involve electron transfer.

sections.title: Energy in Chemical Reactions
body: Every chemical reaction involves energy changes. Exothermic reactions release energy (usually as heat) to the surroundings — the products have lower energy than the reactants, and the difference is released. Combustion, neutralization, and many oxidation reactions are exothermic. Endothermic reactions absorb energy from the surroundings — the products have higher energy than the reactants. Photosynthesis, dissolving many salts, and thermal decomposition are endothermic.

Activation energy is the minimum energy required to initiate a chemical reaction — the energy needed to break existing bonds before new ones can form. Even exothermic reactions require activation energy to get started. Temperature increases reaction rates because more molecules have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the activation energy barrier (the Arrhenius equation).

sections.title: Catalysts and Enzymes
body: A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction by providing an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy, without being consumed in the reaction. Industrial catalysts (platinum, palladium, zeolites) are enormously economically important — the Haber-Bosch process for ammonia synthesis uses an iron catalyst, and catalytic converters in car exhaust systems use platinum and palladium. Enzymes are biological catalysts — protein molecules that accelerate biochemical reactions by factors of millions to billions, making the chemistry of life possible at body temperature.

sections.title: Reaction Rates and Kinetics
body: Chemical kinetics studies the rates of chemical reactions and the factors affecting them. Reaction rate increases with temperature (more energetic collisions), concentration (more frequent collisions), surface area (more reaction sites available), and the presence of catalysts. Pressure affects reactions involving gases.

The rate law expresses the mathematical relationship between reactant concentrations and reaction rate. The order of a reaction with respect to each reactant is determined experimentally. Half-life — the time required for half of a reactant to be consumed — is particularly important in radioactive decay and pharmacokinetics (drug metabolism).

sections.title: Chemical Equilibrium
body: Many reactions are reversible — the forward and reverse reactions occur simultaneously. Chemical equilibrium is the state where the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal and the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant (though not necessarily equal). The equilibrium constant (K) expresses the ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium.

Le Chatelier's Principle states that if a stress (change in concentration, temperature, or pressure) is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system will shift to partially counteract the stress. This principle has enormous practical importance: the Haber process for ammonia production uses high pressure (pushing equilibrium toward products) and moderate temperature (balancing yield and reaction rate).

sections.title: Acids, Bases, and pH
body: Acids are substances that donate protons (H⁺ ions) in solution or accept electron pairs; bases accept protons or donate electron pairs. The pH scale (0–14) measures hydrogen ion concentration: pH 7 is neutral; below 7 is acidic; above 7 is basic (alkaline). Each unit change represents a tenfold change in H⁺ concentration. Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄) fully dissociate; weak acids (acetic acid, carbonic acid) partially dissociate.

Acid-base chemistry is fundamental in biology (blood pH is tightly regulated between 7.35–7.45), industry (sulfuric acid is the world's most produced industrial chemical), food science, environmental science (ocean acidification, acid rain), and medicine (antacids, pH-sensitive drug delivery).

Science.summary: The periodic table of elements is a tabular arrangement of the chemical elements organized by atomic number (number of protons), electron configuration, and recurring chemical properties. It is the most powerful organizing tool in chemistry, revealing the underlying order in the diversity of matter and predicting properties of elements based on their position.
Science.title: The Periodic Table of Elements
Science.subtitle: Chemistry's master organizer — a map of all matter arranged by atomic structure

sections.title: Overview
body: The periodic table of elements is a tabular arrangement of the chemical elements organized by atomic number (number of protons), electron configuration, and recurring chemical properties. It is the most powerful organizing tool in chemistry, revealing the underlying order in the diversity of matter and predicting properties of elements based on their position.

The periodic table was conceived by Russian chemist Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869, who arranged the 63 known elements by atomic mass and noticed periodic repetition of properties. He brilliantly left gaps for undiscovered elements and predicted their properties with remarkable accuracy — three of his predicted elements (gallium, scandium, and germanium) were discovered within 15 years with properties matching his predictions.

sections.title: Structure of the Table
body: Elements are arranged in rows (periods) and columns (groups). A period corresponds to a set of elements having the same highest electron energy shell. Properties change systematically across a period. A group contains elements with the same number of valence electrons (outermost shell electrons) and therefore similar chemical properties.

Moving across a period, atomic radius generally decreases (more protons attract electrons more strongly), ionization energy increases, and electronegativity increases. Moving down a group, atomic radius increases (more electron shells), ionization energy decreases, and metallic character increases. These trends allow prediction of chemical behavior from position alone.

sections.title: Groups and Their Properties
body: Group 1 (alkali metals): lithium, sodium, potassium, etc. — soft, highly reactive metals that react vigorously with water. Group 2 (alkaline earth metals): beryllium, magnesium, calcium — harder and less reactive than Group 1. Group 17 (halogens): fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine — highly reactive nonmetals that readily form salts with metals. Group 18 (noble gases): helium, neon, argon, etc. — colorless, odorless, extremely unreactive due to full valence electron shells.

The transition metals (Groups 3–12) form a central block including iron, copper, gold, silver, and platinum. They are typically hard, high-melting, and can form colored ions in solution. They are the backbone of industrial metallurgy. The lanthanides and actinides are f-block elements placed below the main table; lanthanides are rare earth elements crucial for electronics and green energy technology; actinides include uranium and plutonium.

sections.title: The Atomic Theory Behind the Table
body: The periodic table's structure is explained by quantum mechanics. Each period corresponds to filling a new principal electron shell. Periods 1–2 fill s and p orbitals; Period 4 onward introduces d-orbital (transition metal) filling; Period 6 introduces f-orbital (lanthanide) filling. The chemical properties of elements are determined by the number of valence electrons, which repeats with a period of 2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32 as shells are filled — explaining the periodic law.

sections.title: Metals, Nonmetals, and Metalloids
body: About 75–80% of the elements are metals — characterized by metallic luster, high electrical and thermal conductivity, malleability, ductility, and tendency to lose electrons in chemical reactions. The bulk of the periodic table to the left and bottom is occupied by metals.

Nonmetals are found in the upper right of the table. They are poor conductors, often gaseous or brittle solids at room temperature, and tend to gain electrons. Key nonmetals include carbon (the basis of organic chemistry), nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur — the elements of life. Metalloids (silicon, germanium, arsenic, etc.) have intermediate properties and are critical as semiconductors in electronics.

sections.title: Isotopes and Nuclear Properties
body: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same atomic number, same chemical properties) with different numbers of neutrons and therefore different atomic masses. Many elements have multiple stable isotopes; others have radioactive isotopes (radioisotopes) that decay by emitting radiation.

Radioisotopes have important applications: radiocarbon dating (carbon-14) dates organic materials up to ~50,000 years old; uranium-lead dating ages rocks billions of years old; radioactive isotopes are used in medical diagnostics (PET scans, thyroid imaging), radiation therapy, and nuclear power. Elements 93–118 (transuranium elements) are all synthetic, produced in nuclear reactors or particle accelerators, and are radioactive with short half-lives.

Science.summary: Nanotechnology is the science, engineering, and application of materials and devices with structures and components at the nanoscale — typically 1 to 100 nanometers. At this scale, the properties of materials can differ dramatically from their bulk behavior: quantum mechanical effects become significant, the ratio of surface atoms to bulk atoms is enormous, and entirely new phenomena emerge that can be exploited for transformative applications.
Science.title: Nanotechnology
Science.subtitle: Engineering at the atomic scale — manipulating matter one nanometer at a time

sections.title: Overview
body: Nanotechnology is the science, engineering, and application of materials and devices with structures and components at the nanoscale — typically 1 to 100 nanometers. At this scale, the properties of materials can differ dramatically from their bulk behavior: quantum mechanical effects become significant, the ratio of surface atoms to bulk atoms is enormous, and entirely new phenomena emerge that can be exploited for transformative applications.

Physicist Richard Feynman planted the conceptual seed of nanotechnology in his 1959 lecture 'There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom', envisioning the direct manipulation of individual atoms. The field has since grown from a visionary concept to a major research area with applications across medicine, electronics, energy, materials science, and environmental remediation.

sections.title: Why Nanoscale is Special
body: At the nanoscale, materials exhibit unique properties that differ from both individual atoms and bulk matter. Quantum confinement effects alter electrical and optical properties — gold nanoparticles appear red or purple rather than gold depending on their size. The surface area-to-volume ratio is enormous, making nanoparticles extraordinarily reactive. The behavior of matter transitions from classical to quantum mechanical.

Carbon at the nanoscale illustrates this dramatically: graphite (bulk carbon) is soft and conducts electricity; diamond is extremely hard and insulating; graphene (a single atomic layer of carbon) is the strongest material ever measured and a superior conductor; carbon nanotubes have remarkable strength, electrical, and thermal properties. All are pure carbon — their properties differ because of nanoscale structure alone.

sections.title: Carbon Nanomaterials
body: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are cylindrical sheets of graphene rolled into tubes, with diameters of 1–50 nm and lengths that can reach centimeters. They are approximately 100 times stronger than steel at one-sixth the density, have excellent electrical conductivity (comparable to copper or semiconductors, depending on chirality), and exceptional thermal conductivity. Applications include composite materials, electronics, and drug delivery.

Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, was isolated in 2004 by Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov (Nobel Prize, 2010) using adhesive tape. It is the strongest material ever tested, an excellent conductor of heat and electricity, nearly transparent, and impermeable to all gases. Potential applications include flexible electronics, water filtration membranes, and energy storage.

sections.title: Quantum Dots
body: Quantum dots are semiconductor nanocrystals (typically 2–10 nm) whose optical and electronic properties depend on their size due to quantum confinement. By tuning the size, researchers can produce quantum dots that emit any color of light with high efficiency and color purity. They are already used in QLED television displays and show promise for solar cells, bioimaging, and quantum computing. Their potential toxicity in biomedical applications requires careful evaluation.

sections.title: Nanomedicine
body: Nanomedicine applies nanotechnology to healthcare. Nanoparticles can be engineered to carry drugs directly to cancer cells, reducing side effects and improving efficacy compared to conventional chemotherapy. The COVID-19 mRNA vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna) use lipid nanoparticles — nanotechnology — to deliver fragile mRNA into cells without degradation.

Nanoparticle-based imaging contrast agents improve the sensitivity and resolution of MRI, CT, and other diagnostic modalities. Iron oxide nanoparticles can be guided to tumors by external magnetic fields for both imaging and thermal therapy. Researchers are developing 'smart' nanoparticles that respond to environmental triggers (pH, temperature, enzymes) to release their payload specifically at the disease site.

sections.title: Nanotechnology in Electronics
body: The semiconductor industry has been practicing nanotechnology for decades — modern transistors in processors have feature sizes of 3–5 nm (smaller than most viruses). Continued miniaturization following Moore's Law faces fundamental physical limits as transistors approach atomic dimensions; quantum tunneling causes unwanted current leakage below ~5 nm.

Molecular electronics — using individual molecules as electronic components — is an active research frontier. Nanowire transistors, carbon nanotube transistors, and single-electron transistors represent potential successors to silicon CMOS technology. Quantum computing, which explicitly exploits quantum mechanical superposition and entanglement, may ultimately supersede classical computing for certain applications.

sections.title: Safety and Ethical Concerns
body: The novel properties of nanomaterials that make them useful also raise safety concerns. Nanoparticles can penetrate biological membranes, pass through the blood-brain barrier, and accumulate in organs. Some nanoparticles (certain carbon nanotubes, titanium dioxide, zinc oxide) have shown toxicity in cell and animal studies. Comprehensive nanotoxicology research is essential to ensure safe development.

Environmental release of engineered nanomaterials — through manufacturing, product use, and waste disposal — is a growing concern. Regulatory frameworks for nanomaterials are still developing. Broader societal issues raised by nanotechnology include equity of access to benefits, intellectual property, and long-term implications of capabilities like molecular-scale surveillance or enhanced weaponry.

Science.summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the field of computer science dedicated to creating systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence — perception, language understanding, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, and creativity. From its formal founding at the 1956 Dartmouth Conference, AI has undergone multiple cycles of optimism and 'AI winters' before the current era of rapid progress driven by machine learning and massive computational resources.
Science.title: Artificial Intelligence
Science.subtitle: Machine minds — from rule-based systems to deep learning and the age of large language models

sections.title: Overview
body: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the field of computer science dedicated to creating systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence — perception, language understanding, reasoning, learning, problem-solving, and creativity. From its formal founding at the 1956 Dartmouth Conference, AI has undergone multiple cycles of optimism and 'AI winters' before the current era of rapid progress driven by machine learning and massive computational resources.

Modern AI is dominated by machine learning (ML) — systems that improve through experience with data rather than explicit programming — and particularly by deep learning: artificial neural networks with many layers that learn hierarchical representations of data. The convergence of large datasets, powerful GPUs, and architectural innovations has produced systems of remarkable capability across language, vision, science, and strategy.

sections.title: History of AI
body: Alan Turing's 1950 paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' proposed the Turing Test as a measure of machine intelligence. The Dartmouth Conference of 1956 formally launched AI as a field. Early AI focused on symbolic AI — representing knowledge in logical rules and searching through possibilities. Programs like ELIZA (1966, simulating a therapist) and early chess programs showed promise but hit fundamental limitations.

Two AI winters (late 1970s, late 1980s) followed periods of over-promised and underdelivered progress. Expert systems — large hand-crafted rule bases in specific domains — enjoyed success in the 1980s but could not scale. The machine learning revolution, accelerated by the internet-era data explosion and GPU computing, reignited the field from the 2000s onward.

sections.title: Machine Learning
body: Machine learning algorithms enable computers to learn from data without being explicitly programmed. Supervised learning trains on labeled input-output pairs to predict outputs for new inputs (image classification, spam detection, credit scoring). Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection). Reinforcement learning trains agents to maximize rewards through trial and error in an environment (game playing, robotics).

Classical ML algorithms include decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, k-nearest neighbors, and logistic regression. These remain widely used for structured data problems. Feature engineering — manually designing informative input representations — was a major bottleneck that deep learning largely overcame by learning representations automatically from raw data.

sections.title: Deep Learning and Neural Networks
body: Deep learning uses artificial neural networks inspired loosely by the brain's structure: interconnected layers of nodes (neurons) that transform inputs through learned weights.
