In 2012, AlexNet — a deep convolutional neural network — dramatically outperformed all competitors at the ImageNet computer vision challenge, triggering the deep learning revolution. Since then, deep learning has achieved human-level or superhuman performance in image recognition, speech recognition, language translation, protein structure prediction (AlphaFold), and game playing (AlphaGo, AlphaZero).

sections.title: Large Language Models
body: Large Language Models (LLMs) are deep learning models trained on vast text corpora to understand and generate human language. The transformer architecture (introduced by Vaswani et al. in 2017) underpins virtually all modern LLMs, using attention mechanisms that model relationships between all words in a sequence simultaneously. GPT-3 (2020), GPT-4, Google's Gemini, Anthropic's Claude, and Meta's LLaMA are prominent examples.

LLMs exhibit remarkable emergent capabilities — abilities not explicitly trained for — including reasoning, code generation, mathematical problem solving, and multilingual understanding. Their deployment in conversational AI assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) has made advanced AI accessible to hundreds of millions of people. Questions about accuracy, hallucination, bias, and appropriate use are central to ongoing development.

sections.title: AI in Science and Medicine
body: AlphaFold (DeepMind, 2020–2021) solved the 50-year-old protein folding problem — predicting the 3D structure of proteins from their amino acid sequences — with stunning accuracy, revolutionizing structural biology and drug discovery. Its predictions for virtually all known proteins have been made publicly available, accelerating research across biology and medicine.

AI is transforming medical imaging (detecting cancers in mammograms and CT scans with radiologist-level accuracy), drug discovery (predicting molecular properties and interactions, designing novel molecules), genomics (identifying disease-associated genetic variants), and clinical decision support. In materials science, AI is accelerating the discovery of new materials for batteries, solar cells, and semiconductors.

sections.title: AI Safety and Ethics
body: As AI systems become more capable and widely deployed, questions of safety, fairness, and alignment have become central to the field. Bias in AI systems — reflecting biases in training data — can perpetuate and amplify discrimination in hiring, lending, healthcare, and criminal justice. Explainability — understanding why AI systems make specific decisions — is critical for high-stakes applications.

AI safety research focuses on ensuring that powerful AI systems reliably pursue intended goals and remain under appropriate human control. The rapid capability gains of recent years have intensified debate about risks from advanced AI. Organizations like Anthropic, DeepMind, and OpenAI have active safety research programs, and governments worldwide are developing regulatory frameworks for AI.

Science.summary: Biotechnology is the broad field of using biological systems, living organisms, or derivatives thereof to develop or make products and processes for specific use. In the broadest sense, humans have practiced biotechnology for millennia — selectively breeding crops and livestock, and using microorganisms for fermentation to produce bread, beer, wine, cheese, and vinegar.
Science.title: Biotechnology
Science.subtitle: Harnessing living systems — from ancient fermentation to gene editing and synthetic biology

sections.title: Overview
body: Biotechnology is the broad field of using biological systems, living organisms, or derivatives thereof to develop or make products and processes for specific use. In the broadest sense, humans have practiced biotechnology for millennia — selectively breeding crops and livestock, and using microorganisms for fermentation to produce bread, beer, wine, cheese, and vinegar.

Modern biotechnology, emerging from the recombinant DNA revolution of the 1970s, encompasses genetic engineering, cell culture, biopharmaceuticals, agricultural biotechnology, industrial biotechnology, and the emerging field of synthetic biology. It has transformed medicine, agriculture, forensics, and industrial production, and promises further revolutionary changes through gene therapy, personalized medicine, and engineered organisms.

sections.title: Recombinant DNA Technology
body: Recombinant DNA technology, developed by Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer in 1973, allows specific DNA sequences from one organism to be inserted into the genome of another — creating organisms with new genetic combinations not found in nature. The technique uses restriction enzymes (molecular scissors that cut DNA at specific sequences) and ligases (molecular glue) to cut and join DNA segments, which are then delivered into host cells via vectors (usually plasmids or viruses).

This technology enabled the first major triumph of modern biotechnology: producing human insulin in bacteria (E. coli) engineered with the human insulin gene. Genentech's recombinant human insulin (Humulin), approved in 1982, replaced insulin extracted from pig and cow pancreases and was safer, more consistent, and scalable. It launched the modern biopharmaceutical industry.

sections.title: Biopharmaceuticals
body: Biopharmaceuticals are drugs produced using biological systems — typically mammalian cells, bacteria, or yeast engineered to produce therapeutic proteins. They include monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) — proteins that bind specifically to target molecules (cancer antigens, inflammatory cytokines, viral proteins), used to treat cancer, autoimmune diseases, and infectious diseases; recombinant hormones and growth factors; blood clotting factors for hemophilia; and vaccines.

Monoclonal antibodies have become the fastest-growing class of drugs. Trastuzumab (Herceptin) treats HER2-positive breast cancer; infliximab (Remicade) treats rheumatoid arthritis and Crohn's disease; pembrolizumab (Keytruda) is an immune checkpoint inhibitor that has transformed cancer treatment. The COVID-19 pandemic produced mRNA vaccines — a new category of biopharmaceutical — at unprecedented speed.

sections.title: Gene Therapy
body: Gene therapy aims to treat disease by introducing, altering, or replacing genetic material within a patient's cells. After early setbacks (a patient death in 1999 due to immune reaction to viral vector), the field has matured dramatically. Approved gene therapies now exist for spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), hereditary blindness (Leber congenital amaurosis), beta-thalassemia, sickle cell disease, and several cancers (CAR-T cell therapies). CRISPR-based therapies offer new possibilities for precise in vivo genome editing.

sections.title: Agricultural Biotechnology
body: Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) are organisms whose genome has been altered using genetic engineering. GM crops engineered for herbicide tolerance (Roundup Ready soybeans), insect resistance (Bt corn and cotton, producing the Bacillus thuringiensis toxin), virus resistance, and nutritional enhancement (Golden Rice, engineered to produce beta-carotene, addressing vitamin A deficiency) are widely grown.

CRISPR is enabling a new generation of crop improvements with fewer regulatory constraints than traditional GMOs — since no foreign DNA is necessarily introduced. Crops being developed include disease-resistant bananas, drought-tolerant wheat, and mushrooms that don't brown. Agricultural biotechnology also encompasses microbial inoculants, biocontrol agents, biofertilizers, and the development of new crop varieties through accelerated breeding.

sections.title: Synthetic Biology
body: Synthetic biology applies engineering principles to biology — designing and constructing new biological parts, devices, and systems, or redesigning existing natural systems for useful purposes. It treats biological components as standardized, interchangeable parts (BioBricks) that can be assembled into circuits, networks, and organisms with designed functions.

Achievements include engineering yeast to produce artemisinin (an antimalarial drug, replacing plant extraction), bacteria that detect and kill cancer cells, cells that function as biosensors for environmental pollutants, and microorganisms that produce biofuels. The J. Craig Venter Institute created the first synthetic bacterial cell with a fully synthetic genome (Syn 3.0, 2016) — a minimal cell with only the genes essential for life.

sections.title: Forensic Biotechnology and DNA Fingerprinting
body: DNA fingerprinting, developed by Alec Jeffreys in 1984, uses the unique pattern of short tandem repeats (STRs) in an individual's genome to produce a genetic profile that (except for identical twins) is unique to each person. It has revolutionized forensic science — used to identify perpetrators, exonerate the wrongly convicted (The Innocence Project has exonerated hundreds using DNA evidence), identify victims, and establish paternity.

Environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis detects the presence of organisms from traces of DNA shed into the environment (water, soil, air). It is transforming ecological monitoring — detecting invasive species, endangered species, and pathogens far more efficiently than traditional survey methods. Metagenomics — sequencing all the DNA in an environmental sample — has revealed extraordinary microbial biodiversity.

Science.summary: Particle physics is the branch of physics that studies the nature of the fundamental constituents of matter and radiation, and the forces governing their interactions. It seeks to answer the deepest questions: what are the irreducible building blocks of the universe? What forces act between them? Why is there matter rather than antimatter? Are the known forces aspects of a single unified force?
Science.title: Particle Physics
Science.subtitle: The quest for the fundamental building blocks of matter and the forces between them

sections.title: Overview
body: Particle physics is the branch of physics that studies the nature of the fundamental constituents of matter and radiation, and the forces governing their interactions. It seeks to answer the deepest questions: what are the irreducible building blocks of the universe? What forces act between them? Why is there matter rather than antimatter? Are the known forces aspects of a single unified force?

The Standard Model of particle physics, developed through the 1960s–1970s and confirmed through decades of experiments, describes 17 fundamental particles and three of the four fundamental forces. It is the most precisely tested theory in physics — predicting quantities that agree with experiment to extraordinary accuracy — yet physicists know it is incomplete.

sections.title: The Standard Model
body: The Standard Model classifies all known fundamental particles into two groups: fermions (matter particles, obeying Fermi-Dirac statistics) and bosons (force-carrying particles, obeying Bose-Einstein statistics). Fermions include quarks (6 flavors, combining to form hadrons like protons and neutrons) and leptons (electrons, muons, tau particles, and their associated neutrinos — 6 types total). Each has an antimatter counterpart.

Bosons mediate the fundamental forces: photons (electromagnetic force), W± and Z bosons (weak nuclear force), gluons (strong nuclear force). The Higgs boson — discovered at CERN's Large Hadron Collider on July 4, 2012 — is associated with the Higgs field that gives other particles their mass. Gravity, described by general relativity, is not included in the Standard Model — its quantum carrier (the graviton) has not been detected.

sections.title: Quarks and the Strong Force
body: Quarks are fundamental particles that combine to form composite particles called hadrons (most importantly protons and neutrons). Quarks come in six 'flavors': up, down, charm, strange, top, and bottom. Protons are made of two up quarks and one down quark (uud); neutrons of one up and two downs (udd). Quarks carry fractional electric charges (+2/3 or -1/3) and are confined — they cannot be observed in isolation.

The strong nuclear force, mediated by gluons, binds quarks together inside protons and neutrons, and binds protons and neutrons together in atomic nuclei. It is the strongest of all forces but has very short range (confined to the nucleus). Quantum chromodynamics (QCD) is the theory of the strong force — quarks carry a property called 'color charge' (not visible color) that is exchanged through gluons.

sections.title: The Higgs Boson
body: The Higgs boson is the quantum excitation of the Higgs field — a scalar field that permeates all of space. As particles move through this field, they interact with it to varying degrees, and this interaction is what gives them mass (massless particles like photons do not interact with the Higgs field). The Higgs mechanism solved the critical problem of why W and Z bosons are massive while photons are massless. Its 2012 discovery completed the Standard Model and earned François Englert and Peter Higgs the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physics.

sections.title: Antimatter
body: For every fundamental particle, there is a corresponding antiparticle with the same mass but opposite quantum numbers (charge, etc.). When a particle meets its antiparticle, they annihilate, converting their mass entirely into energy (E = mc²). The positron (antielectron), discovered by Carl Anderson in 1932, was the first antiparticle found.

The matter-antimatter asymmetry is one of the deepest unsolved problems in physics. The Big Bang should have produced equal amounts of matter and antimatter, which would have annihilated leaving nothing but radiation. Yet the universe consists of matter. Slightly more matter than antimatter must have been created (a difference of roughly 1 in a billion), but the physical mechanism explaining this asymmetry is not fully understood.

sections.title: Beyond the Standard Model
body: The Standard Model is demonstrably incomplete. It does not include gravity. It does not explain dark matter (which constitutes ~27% of the universe's mass) or dark energy (~68%). It does not explain the matter-antimatter asymmetry. Neutrino oscillations — the phenomenon where neutrinos change flavor as they travel — imply neutrinos have mass, which was not in the original Standard Model.

Leading candidates for beyond-Standard-Model physics include supersymmetry (SUSY), which predicts a partner particle for every known particle; extra dimensions; grand unified theories; and string theory. Despite extensive searches at the LHC, no clear evidence for supersymmetry or other new physics has emerged above the Higgs — a profound puzzle for theorists.

Science.summary: Astronomy is one of the oldest sciences, concerned with the observation and cataloguing of celestial objects and phenomena — stars, planets, moons, galaxies, nebulae, black holes, and the large-scale structure of the universe. Astrophysics is a branch of astronomy that applies the principles of physics and chemistry to understand the physical nature, origin, evolution, and ultimate fate of astronomical objects and the universe itself.
Science.title: Astronomy vs Astrophysics
Science.subtitle: Two branches of the same cosmic science — observing the universe and understanding its physics

sections.title: Overview
body: Astronomy is one of the oldest sciences, concerned with the observation and cataloguing of celestial objects and phenomena — stars, planets, moons, galaxies, nebulae, black holes, and the large-scale structure of the universe. Astrophysics is a branch of astronomy that applies the principles of physics and chemistry to understand the physical nature, origin, evolution, and ultimate fate of astronomical objects and the universe itself.

In practice, the boundary between astronomy and astrophysics is largely historical — modern astronomers virtually always engage in astrophysical interpretation of their observations, and astrophysicists depend on observational data. Together they constitute a single enterprise: using light and other signals from the cosmos to understand the universe.

sections.title: History of Astronomy
body: Ancient Babylonians systematically recorded celestial events and developed the zodiac. Greek astronomers developed geometric models of the solar system — culminating in Ptolemy's geocentric model (2nd century CE), which dominated for 1,400 years. Nicolaus Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model in 1543. Tycho Brahe compiled the most accurate pre-telescope planetary observations. Johannes Kepler derived his three laws of planetary motion from Brahe's data (1609–1619).

Galileo Galilei was the first to systematically use a telescope for astronomical observation (1609), discovering Jupiter's four large moons, the phases of Venus (confirming the heliocentric model), and the composition of the Milky Way from individual stars. Isaac Newton unified celestial and terrestrial mechanics with his Law of Universal Gravitation (1687), showing that the same force governing falling apples also keeps planets in orbit.

sections.title: The Tools of Astronomy
body: Telescopes are the fundamental tool of astronomy. Optical telescopes collect and focus visible light. Radio telescopes detect longer-wavelength radio waves — revealing phenomena invisible to optical telescopes, including pulsars, quasars, the cosmic microwave background, and molecular clouds. Space telescopes observe wavelengths absorbed by Earth's atmosphere: ultraviolet (Hubble), X-ray (Chandra), gamma-ray (Fermi), and infrared (Spitzer, JWST).

Spectroscopy is arguably the most powerful astrophysical technique — analyzing the spectrum of light from a celestial object reveals its chemical composition, temperature, density, magnetic field, velocity (through Doppler shift), and redshift (distance). By analyzing spectral lines, astronomers can determine what stars and galaxies are made of from billions of light-years away.

sections.title: Multi-Messenger Astronomy
body: Traditional astronomy uses electromagnetic radiation. Multi-messenger astronomy combines multiple signals: gravitational waves (detected by LIGO/Virgo from merging black holes and neutron stars), cosmic rays (high-energy particles), and neutrinos (nearly massless particles from supernovae and other extreme events). The 2017 detection of a neutron star merger in both gravitational waves and electromagnetic radiation marked the dawn of multi-messenger astronomy — enabling unprecedented insights into the most extreme events in the universe.

sections.title: Key Areas of Astrophysics
body: Stellar astrophysics studies the structure, evolution, and death of stars — using theoretical models of nuclear fusion, equation of state, and radiation transport validated by spectroscopic observations. Solar physics studies our nearest star in detail, including solar flares, coronal mass ejections, and the solar wind.

Galactic astrophysics studies the structure, formation, and dynamics of galaxies. Extragalactic astronomy studies galaxies, galaxy clusters, and the large-scale structure of the universe. High-energy astrophysics uses X-ray and gamma-ray observations to study the most energetic phenomena — black holes, neutron stars, supernovae, gamma-ray bursts. Cosmology studies the origin, structure, evolution, and ultimate fate of the universe as a whole.

sections.title: Cosmology — The Science of the Universe
body: Cosmology addresses the largest-scale questions: How did the universe begin? How did it evolve? What is it made of? What is its geometry and fate? The Big Bang theory — supported by the cosmic microwave background, the abundance of light elements, and the observed expansion of the universe — describes the universe's evolution from an extremely hot, dense initial state 13.8 billion years ago.

The standard cosmological model (ΛCDM — Lambda Cold Dark Matter) describes a universe composed of ~5% ordinary matter, ~27% dark matter, and ~68% dark energy, with geometry that is flat to high precision. This model successfully explains the universe's large-scale structure, the CMB, and the history of cosmic expansion. Yet ΛCDM contains two completely unexplained components — dark matter and dark energy — pointing to deep gaps in understanding.

Science.summary: Neuroscience is the scientific study of the nervous system — the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves — and the biological basis of behavior, cognition, emotion, and consciousness. It is a highly interdisciplinary field, drawing on biology, chemistry, physics, mathematics, psychology, medicine, computer science, and philosophy. It encompasses molecular and cellular studies of individual neurons to large-scale investigations of brain networks and behavior.
Science.title: Neuroscience
Science.subtitle: The science of the mind — understanding the brain, neurons, and the biology of thought

sections.title: Overview
body: Neuroscience is the scientific study of the nervous system — the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves — and the biological basis of behavior, cognition, emotion, and consciousness. It is a highly interdisciplinary field, drawing on biology, chemistry, physics, mathematics, psychology, medicine, computer science, and philosophy. It encompasses molecular and cellular studies of individual neurons to large-scale investigations of brain networks and behavior.

The brain is the most complex object known in the universe. Its approximately 86 billion neurons form an estimated 100 trillion synaptic connections, organized into specialized regions and networks that process sensory information, coordinate movement, generate language, store memories, experience emotions, and give rise to consciousness. Understanding how this biological machinery produces mind and experience is the central challenge of neuroscience.

sections.title: Neurons and Neural Signaling
body: Neurons are the specialized cells of the nervous system. A typical neuron has a cell body (soma), dendrites (receiving branches that collect inputs from other neurons), and an axon (a long projection that transmits signals to other neurons or muscles). Axons are often covered by a myelin sheath — a fatty insulating layer that dramatically increases conduction speed and is produced by oligodendrocytes (in the CNS) or Schwann cells (in the PNS).

Neurons communicate through electrical and chemical signals. An action potential — a rapid, all-or-nothing electrical pulse (~70 mV reversal in membrane potential) — travels along the axon when membrane depolarization reaches a threshold. At the synapse (the junction between neurons), the electrical signal triggers the release of chemical neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft. These bind to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron, generating excitatory or inhibitory currents.

sections.title: Neurotransmitters
body: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses. Glutamate is the main excitatory neurotransmitter; GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) is the main inhibitory one. Dopamine is involved in reward, motivation, motor control, and is implicated in addiction and Parkinson's disease. Serotonin modulates mood, appetite, and sleep; dysregulation is implicated in depression and anxiety. Acetylcholine mediates muscle contraction and is involved in memory and attention.

Norepinephrine (noradrenaline) mediates the fight-or-flight response and alertness. Endorphins are endogenous opioids — pain-relief and reward molecules. Many drugs act by mimicking, blocking, or altering the activity of specific neurotransmitters: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) for depression, L-DOPA for Parkinson's, methadone for opioid addiction, ketamine as an anesthetic and antidepressant.

sections.title: Neuroplasticity
body: Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to reorganize itself — forming new synaptic connections, strengthening existing ones, and in some cases growing new neurons — in response to learning, experience, injury, or sensory deprivation. 'Neurons that fire together, wire together' (Hebbian plasticity) describes how repeated co-activation strengthens synaptic connections. Neuroplasticity is the basis for learning, memory formation, recovery from brain injury, and the effectiveness of rehabilitation after stroke. The adult brain retains far more plasticity than was once believed.

sections.title: Brain Regions and Functions
body: The cerebral cortex — the outer, folded layer of gray matter — is the seat of higher cognitive functions: perception, voluntary movement, language, reasoning, memory, and consciousness. It is divided into four lobes: frontal (executive function, motor control, language production), parietal (sensory integration, spatial awareness), temporal (auditory processing, memory, language comprehension), and occipital (visual processing).

The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and procedural learning. The limbic system (hippocampus, amygdala, hypothalamus) mediates emotion, memory consolidation, and survival drives. The brainstem controls vital automatic functions (breathing, heart rate, swallowing). The basal ganglia are involved in motor control, habit formation, and reward processing — key targets of dysfunction in Parkinson's disease and addiction.

sections.title: Neurological and Psychiatric Disorders
body: Neurological disorders are caused by structural or functional abnormalities of the nervous system. Alzheimer's disease — the most common neurodegenerative disease — is characterized by accumulation of amyloid plaques and tau tangles in the brain, causing progressive memory loss and cognitive decline. Parkinson's disease involves loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, causing tremors and movement difficulties. Stroke — disruption of blood supply to part of the brain — kills neurons within minutes.

Psychiatric disorders (depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, ADHD) involve dysregulation of brain chemistry and circuit function. The boundary between neurological and psychiatric disorders is increasingly blurred as the biological underpinnings of psychiatric conditions are better understood. Despite enormous research investment, treatments for most neurological disorders remain largely symptomatic rather than curative.

sections.title: Consciousness and the Brain
body: Consciousness — subjective experience, the sense of 'what it is like' to be a thinking, feeling creature — is arguably the deepest problem in neuroscience. The 'hard problem of consciousness' (David Chalmers, 1995) asks why and how physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience. No current neuroscientific theory fully explains why there is something it is like to see red, feel pain, or experience the passage of time.

Competing theories include Integrated Information Theory (IIT), Global Workspace Theory (GWT), Higher-Order Theories, and Predictive Processing frameworks. New tools — optogenetics, high-density neural recording, brain organoids — are generating unprecedented data about neural correlates of consciousness. The fundamental question of machine consciousness — whether artificial systems could be conscious — has profound implications for AI development and ethics.

Science.summary: Renewable energy technologies harness energy from naturally replenished resources — sunlight, wind, flowing water, Earth's heat, and biological matter — to generate electricity, heat, and fuel. They are the cornerstone of the global energy transition away from fossil fuels, essential for limiting climate change while meeting growing global energy demand.
Science.title: Renewable Energy Technologies
Science.subtitle: Powering the future — solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, and the energy transition

sections.title: Overview
body: Renewable energy technologies harness energy from naturally replenished resources — sunlight, wind, flowing water, Earth's heat, and biological matter — to generate electricity, heat, and fuel. They are the cornerstone of the global energy transition away from fossil fuels, essential for limiting climate change while meeting growing global energy demand.

The economics of renewable energy have been transformed over the past decade. Solar photovoltaic and wind power are now the cheapest sources of new electricity generation in most of the world — cheaper than coal, gas, or nuclear — and deployment has accelerated dramatically. In 2023, renewables accounted for about 30% of global electricity generation, with the share growing rapidly.

sections.title: Solar Energy
body: Solar photovoltaic (PV) technology converts sunlight directly to electricity using semiconductor materials (typically silicon) that exhibit the photovoltaic effect. When photons with sufficient energy strike a solar cell, they knock electrons loose, generating a direct current (DC) that is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter. Solar PV is modular — ranging from small rooftop systems to multi-gigawatt utility farms.

The cost of solar PV has declined by over 90% between 2010 and 2023, driven by economies of scale, manufacturing improvements, and learning curve effects. In 2023, solar was the cheapest source of new electricity in most markets. Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) uses mirrors to concentrate sunlight, generate heat, and drive a turbine — with thermal storage enabling dispatchable power even after sunset.

sections.title: Next-Generation Solar
body: Perovskite solar cells — based on a class of materials with an ABX₃ crystal structure — have seen laboratory efficiencies rise from ~4% in 2009 to over 25% in 2023, approaching the best silicon cells. Tandem cells combining perovskite and silicon layers have exceeded 33% efficiency in the lab, approaching the theoretical Shockley-Queisser limit of ~33% for single-junction cells. Organic solar cells, bifacial cells (capturing light from both sides), and building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV — solar cells integrated into windows, roofing, facades) represent further frontiers.

sections.title: Wind Energy
body: Wind turbines convert the kinetic energy of wind into electricity through aerodynamic lift on turbine blades, which rotate a shaft connected to a generator. Modern onshore turbines have rotor diameters of 100–180 m and generate 3–6 MW each. Offshore wind turbines are larger still — the latest offshore models have rotor diameters exceeding 220 m and capacities of 15–22 MW.

Offshore wind is particularly valuable because offshore winds are stronger and more consistent than onshore, and large turbines can be installed without visual or noise impact on communities. The North Sea, Taiwan Strait, US East Coast, and other high-wind offshore regions are seeing rapid development. Wind is now the dominant source of new renewable electricity in many countries including the US, Germany, and the UK.

sections.title: Hydropower and Ocean Energy
body: Hydropower — generating electricity from flowing water — is the world's largest source of renewable electricity (about 16% of global supply), with about 1,400 GW of installed capacity. Large dams (Three Gorges in China — 22.5 GW) provide both power and water storage, but involve significant ecological and social impacts. Run-of-river plants have lower impacts.

Ocean energy technologies are at earlier stages. Tidal power captures the kinetic and potential energy of tides — the tidal barrage at La Rance, France (240 MW) has operated since 1966. Wave energy converters extract energy from ocean surface waves. Ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC) exploits temperature differences between warm surface water and cold deep water. Globally, the ocean energy resource is enormous but challenges of reliability, marine environment durability, and cost remain.

sections.title: Geothermal and Biomass
body: Geothermal energy taps heat from Earth's interior — either high-temperature hydrothermal resources (steam or hot water) for electricity generation, or lower-temperature resources for direct heating. Iceland generates 100% of its electricity and meets most of its heating needs from geothermal and hydropower. Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS) — creating artificial reservoirs by hydraulic fracturing of hot dry rock — could dramatically expand the geothermal resource globally.

Bioenergy uses organic matter (biomass) — wood, agricultural residues, dedicated energy crops, algae, and waste — for heat, electricity, and liquid fuels. When sustainably sourced, biomass is considered low-carbon as the CO₂ released during combustion was recently absorbed by the growing plant. Biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel, renewable diesel, sustainable aviation fuel) are important for transport sectors that are difficult to electrify. Biogas (methane from anaerobic digestion of organic waste) provides renewable natural gas.

sections.title: Energy Storage and Grid Integration
body: The intermittency of solar and wind — they generate only when the sun shines and wind blows — makes energy storage and grid flexibility essential for high-renewable electricity systems. Lithium-ion batteries have rapidly reduced in cost (90% since 2010) and dominate grid-scale storage, with gigawatt-scale battery installations now common. Pumped hydropower storage — pumping water uphill when electricity is cheap, releasing it through turbines when needed — remains the world's largest form of energy storage.

Green hydrogen — produced by electrolysis of water powered by renewable electricity — can store and transport energy at large scale and long duration, and can decarbonize 'hard to abate' sectors like steel, cement, and aviation. Smart grids with advanced sensors, demand response, vehicle-to-grid integration, and digital coordination are needed to balance supply and demand in a highly renewable power system.
